eli3: LlamaIndex (formerly GPT Index) is a Python framework specialised for connecting LLMs to data. Its core strength is RAG: loading documents, indexing them, and retrieving relevant pieces to answer questions. It goes deeper than LangChain on the retrieval and indexing side.

If LangChain is a general LEGO set for LLM apps, LlamaIndex is the specialised data-retrieval set. It has more sophisticated indexing structures (tree indices, keyword indices, knowledge graphs), more retrieval strategies (sub-question decomposition, auto-merging, sentence window), and a dedicated data query language.

As of 2024, LlamaIndex also has agent capabilities (AgentRunner), multi-modal support, and workflows for complex pipelines. But its strongest differentiator remains advanced RAG pipelines for document-heavy applications.

whenWhy.title: Production RAG with advanced retrieval strategies
whenWhy.body: LlamaIndex is the go-to framework when basic top-k semantic search isn't enough. Its advanced retrieval features: auto-merging retrieval (retrieve small chunks, but return their parent nodes for full context), sentence window retrieval (embed sentences, return surrounding paragraph), sub-question decomposition (break complex queries into sub-questions, retrieve for each), recursive retrieval (query an index that returns references to other indices). These strategies significantly improve RAG quality over naive chunking + similarity search.

whenWhy.title: Diverse data source integration
whenWhy.body: LlamaIndex's Reader ecosystem (100+ integrations): PDF, Word, HTML, Notion, Confluence, Slack, Google Drive, GitHub, databases, APIs. Each Reader normalises the source into LlamaIndex's Document format, handling source-specific parsing (PDF formatting, HTML tag stripping, database schema serialisation). For applications that must ingest heterogeneous data sources, LlamaIndex's Reader ecosystem reduces integration time significantly. The data connectors are its most extensive feature.

whenWhy.title: Knowledge graph-based retrieval
whenWhy.body: LlamaIndex's KnowledgeGraphIndex and PropertyGraphIndex extract entities and relationships from documents and store them as a graph (backed by Neo4j, Nebula, or an in-memory graph). At query time, the query is converted to graph traversal, retrieving fact chains rather than passage embeddings. This is more effective than vector search for queries requiring multi-hop reasoning ('Who is the CEO of the company that acquired OpenAI?') where the answer requires chaining relationships across multiple entities.

deepDiveIntro: LlamaIndex was created by Jerry Liu in November 2022 as a simple interface over OpenAI's API for document Q&A. It has since become a comprehensive framework for data-centric LLM applications. Key abstractions: Document (a piece of data with text and metadata), Node (a chunk derived from a Document, the unit of retrieval), Index (a data structure over Nodes for efficient retrieval), QueryEngine (a retrieval + synthesis pipeline over an Index), and AgentRunner (an agent loop over tools, which can include QueryEngines as tools).

LlamaIndex's retrieval architecture separates retrieval from synthesis: a Retriever finds relevant Nodes; a ResponseSynthesiser combines the retrieved Nodes into a final answer. This separation enables swapping retrieval strategies independently of the synthesis step, and enables evaluation of each component independently. The QueryEngine orchestrates the two.

deepDive.heading: Advanced Retrieval Strategies
deepDive.body: Sentence Window Retrieval: embed each sentence individually (high precision), but when a sentence is retrieved, return it along with a window of K surrounding sentences (provides context for synthesis). Auto-Merging Retrieval: build a hierarchical index (sentence → paragraph → document). Retrieve at sentence level; if K out of N sentences from the same parent paragraph are retrieved, return the full paragraph instead. Small-to-Big Retrieval: retrieve small chunks (high recall), then expand each chunk to its parent node (large chunk, more context). Sub-Question Decomposition: given a complex query, generate N sub-questions, retrieve for each, and synthesise a final answer. Best for multi-hop queries. [1]

deepDive.heading: Index Types
deepDive.body: VectorStoreIndex (most common): embeds nodes and stores in a vector DB; retrieval by ANN similarity search. SummaryIndex (formerly ListIndex): stores all nodes in a list; retrieval iterates over all nodes (suitable for small document sets). TreeIndex: builds a tree of summaries from leaf nodes up; retrieval traverses the tree from root to leaf. Useful for very long documents where you want hierarchical summarisation. KeywordTableIndex: extracts keywords from each node; retrieval by keyword intersection with the query. KnowledgeGraphIndex: extracts (entity, relation, entity) triples; retrieval by graph traversal. For most production RAG, VectorStoreIndex + an advanced retrieval strategy is the standard starting point. [1]

deepDive.heading: LlamaIndex vs LangChain for RAG
deepDive.body: LlamaIndex advantages for RAG: deeper retrieval abstractions (more built-in advanced strategies), better multi-index composition (query across multiple indices), more sophisticated evaluation (Ragas integration, TruLens integration). LangChain advantages: broader ecosystem beyond RAG (agents, memory, chains), wider community, more integrations for non-retrieval components. In practice: many teams use LlamaIndex for the retrieval layer and LangChain/LangGraph for the agent orchestration layer, since they can be combined: a LlamaIndex QueryEngine can be wrapped as a LangChain Tool and used inside a LangGraph agent. [1]

takeaways: LlamaIndex specialises in data-centric LLM applications — strongest at advanced RAG and document ingestion. [1]
Advanced retrieval: sentence window, auto-merging, sub-question decomposition — all outperform naive top-k. [1]
100+ Reader integrations: PDF, Notion, Confluence, databases — normalises heterogeneous sources to Document format. [1]
Index types: VectorStoreIndex (default), TreeIndex (hierarchical), KnowledgeGraphIndex (multi-hop reasoning). [1]
Can be combined with LangGraph: wrap a LlamaIndex QueryEngine as a LangChain tool for agent use. [1]

edgeCases.q: When should you build a custom retriever instead of using LlamaIndex's built-in strategies?
edgeCases.a: Build a custom retriever when: (1) Your data has domain-specific structure that built-in strategies don't handle (e.g., financial filings with table data — tables need structured retrieval, not embedding-based); (2) You need retrieval logic that combines multiple signals (recency + semantic similarity + user-specific weighting); (3) The built-in strategies have too many layers of abstraction that prevent fine-tuning for your latency requirements; (4) You need to integrate a proprietary search system. LlamaIndex makes custom retrievers easy: subclass BaseRetriever, implement _retrieve(query_bundle) → list[NodeWithScore], and plug it into any QueryEngine. The rest of the pipeline (synthesis, caching, tracing) works as normal.

edgeCases.q: How do you keep the LlamaIndex index up-to-date as the underlying documents change?
edgeCases.a: Document refresh strategies: (1) Full re-index — delete and rebuild the entire index on a schedule (simplest but expensive for large corpora); (2) Incremental update — track document hashes; re-embed and re-index only documents that have changed. LlamaIndex's RefreshIndex detects stale nodes by comparing stored document hashes to current source hashes; (3) Streaming ingest — for real-time document sources (Slack, email), use event-driven ingestion: a webhook triggers a LlamaIndex ingestion pipeline when new content arrives; (4) Deletion handling — when a source document is deleted, query the index by document_id and delete all its nodes. The ingestion pipeline (SimpleDirectoryReader + IngestionPipeline) handles all four cases with the right configuration.

codeSnapshot.label: llamaindex_rag.py
codeSnapshot.code:
from llama_index.core import VectorStoreIndex, SimpleDirectoryReader
from llama_index.llms.openai import OpenAI
from llama_index.embeddings.openai import OpenAIEmbedding
from llama_index.core import Settings

# 1. Configure global LLM and embedding model
Settings.llm = OpenAI(model="gpt-4o", temperature=0)
Settings.embed_model = OpenAIEmbedding(model="text-embedding-3-small")

# 2. Load documents from a directory
documents = SimpleDirectoryReader("./data").load_data()
print(f"Loaded {len(documents)} documents")

# 3. Build a vector index (embeds nodes and stores in memory)
index = VectorStoreIndex.from_documents(documents)

# 4. Create a query engine with custom retrieval settings
query_engine = index.as_query_engine(
    llm=OpenAI(model="gpt-4o"),
    similarity_top_k=5,          # retrieve top-5 most relevant nodes
    response_mode="compact",     # synthesise into a concise answer
)

# 5. Run a query
response = query_engine.query(
    "What is the main contribution of the paper?"
)
print(response)

# 6. Inspect source nodes (citations)
for i, node in enumerate(response.source_nodes):
    print(f"\n--- Source {i+1} (score: {node.score:.4f}) ---")
    print(f"File: {node.metadata.get('file_name', 'unknown')}")
    print(f"Text: {node.text[:200]}...")

deepDive.heading: Advanced Retrieval Strategies
deepDive.body: Sentence Window Retrieval: embed each sentence individually (high precision), but when a sentence is retrieved, return it along with a window of K surrounding sentences (provides context for synthesis). Auto-Merging Retrieval: build a hierarchical index (sentence → paragraph → document). Retrieve at sentence level; if K out of N sentences from the same parent paragraph are retrieved, return the full paragraph instead. Small-to-Big Retrieval: retrieve small chunks (high recall), then expand each chunk to its parent node (large chunk, more context). Sub-Question Decomposition: given a complex query, generate N sub-questions, retrieve for each, and synthesise a final answer. Best for multi-hop queries. [1]

deepDive.heading: Index Types
deepDive.body: VectorStoreIndex (most common): embeds nodes and stores in a vector DB; retrieval by ANN similarity search. SummaryIndex (formerly ListIndex): stores all nodes in a list; retrieval iterates over all nodes (suitable for small document sets). TreeIndex: builds a tree of summaries from leaf nodes up; retrieval traverses the tree from root to leaf. Useful for very long documents where you want hierarchical summarisation. KeywordTableIndex: extracts keywords from each node; retrieval by keyword intersection with the query. KnowledgeGraphIndex: extracts (entity, relation, entity) triples; retrieval by graph traversal. For most production RAG, VectorStoreIndex + an advanced retrieval strategy is the standard starting point. [1]

deepDive.heading: LlamaIndex vs LangChain for RAG
deepDive.body: LlamaIndex advantages for RAG: deeper retrieval abstractions (more built-in advanced strategies), better multi-index composition (query across multiple indices), more sophisticated evaluation (Ragas integration, TruLens integration). LangChain advantages: broader ecosystem beyond RAG (agents, memory, chains), wider community, more integrations for non-retrieval components. In practice: many teams use LlamaIndex for the retrieval layer and LangChain/LangGraph for the agent orchestration layer, since they can be combined: a LlamaIndex QueryEngine can be wrapped as a LangChain Tool and used inside a LangGraph agent. [1]

deepDive.heading: Node Postprocessors and Query Transformations
deepDive.body: LlamaIndex's postprocessor pipeline runs after retrieval and before synthesis, allowing retrieved nodes to be filtered, reranked, or augmented. SimilarityPostprocessor removes nodes below a score threshold (e.g., similarity_cutoff=0.75), ensuring only high-confidence matches reach the LLM — important for reducing hallucination from marginally relevant context. KeywordNodePostprocessor filters nodes that must (or must not) contain specific keywords, useful for domain-specific guardrails. LLMRerank is the most powerful: it sends retrieved nodes to an LLM (or cross-encoder model) with the original query and asks it to score relevance from 1–10, then re-sorts and truncates the list. Cross-encoder reranking consistently outperforms bi-encoder retrieval for final-stage precision at the cost of additional LLM calls. On the query transformation side, HyDE (Hypothetical Document Embeddings) generates a hypothetical answer to the query first, then uses the hypothetical answer's embedding for retrieval — this closes the semantic gap between short questions and long document passages. Decompose query transformations break a complex multi-part question into independent sub-questions, retrieve for each, and synthesise a combined answer. Step-back prompting reformulates the query to a more abstract level ('What general principle does this question relate to?') before retrieval, improving recall for domain-specific queries. [1]

deepDive.heading: LlamaIndex vs LangChain: When to Use Each
deepDive.body: LlamaIndex and LangChain overlap significantly but have distinct strengths. LlamaIndex excels at data indexing and retrieval: it offers richer index types (VectorStoreIndex, KnowledgeGraphIndex, SummaryIndex, TreeIndex), more sophisticated retrieval strategies (auto-merging, sentence window, sub-question decomposition), and deeper evaluation integrations (TruLens, Ragas). Its data connector ecosystem (100+ SimpleDirectoryReader plugins) makes ingesting heterogeneous sources straightforward. For Q&A over large document collections, LlamaIndex is the more capable choice out of the box. LangChain excels at composing arbitrary components into pipelines and agent loops: its LCEL pipe operator makes it easy to wire together retrievers, LLMs, output parsers, and custom logic; LangGraph extends this to stateful cyclic workflows with checkpointing and human-in-the-loop. For multi-step agentic workflows that happen to include retrieval, LangChain/LangGraph is the stronger orchestration layer. The frameworks are complementary: wrap a LlamaIndex QueryEngine as a LangChain Tool (from llama_index.core.tools import QueryEngineTool; from langchain.tools import Tool), then use it inside a LangGraph ReAct agent. This gives you LlamaIndex's retrieval quality with LangChain's agent control flow and LangSmith's observability. [1]
